notes: Cross-reactivity with penicillin allergy ~1-3%. Higher with 1st-gen cephalosporins. Skin testing recommended.
First-generation cephalosporin. Higher cross-reactivity (~2%) with penicillin allergy than later generations.
Cross-reactivity within fluoroquinolone class ~10%. Avoid all fluoroquinolones if prior anaphylaxis.
Cross-reactivity within SSRI class is rare but documented. Usually manifests as rash/urticaria.
Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD) affects ~7% of asthmatics. All COX-1 inhibitors cross-react. Celecoxib usually safe.
Sulfonamide antibiotic allergy does NOT cross-react with non-antibiotic sulfonamides (thiazides, furosemide). Different mechanism.
True metformin allergy is extremely rare. Most reported reactions are GI intolerance, not allergy. No cross-reactive class.
True opioid allergy is rare (~2%). Most reactions are pseudo-allergic (direct mast cell degranulation). Cross-reactivity varies by chemical subclass.
Cross-reactivity within tetracycline class is expected. Avoid all tetracyclines if prior allergic reaction.
Prior contrast reaction: 17-35% repeat reaction risk. Premedicate with steroids + antihistamines. Consider gadolinium-based alternative if safe.
Not a true allergy cross-reactivity — these drugs cause disulfiram-like reactions with alcohol (aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibition).
Both are folate antagonists. Cross-reactivity is theoretical but poorly studied. Desensitization protocols available.
